The Glebe Island Bridge (1903) is one of the oldest electrically operated swing bridges in the world — still part of the skyline

Site of the first successful Green Ban (1970s) — residents and unions stopped a freeway from demolishing the historic terrace streetscape

Once working-class, became the heart of Sydney's intellectual and bohemian scene in the 60s and 70s — activists, artists, academics

Glebe

City of Sydney

Inner West

April 2026

Portrait

Glebe is a place where Sydney's Victorian soul refuses to be quieted by the hum of the modern city. Walking down Glebe Point Road feels like flipping through a dusty, well-loved novel — one minute you are passing a gothic-style mansion like the eerie Sze Yup Temple, and the next, swept up in the aroma of artisanal sourdough and old paper from a corner bookstore.

It is a neighbourhood that wears its history like a badge of honour, where grand 19th-century terraces with intricate iron lacework stand defiantly against the backdrop of the sleek CBD skyline.

 

The true magic of Glebe lies in its bohemian pulse, kept alive by the students of Sydney University and the grizzled poets who never left. Every Saturday the local school transforms into the Glebe Markets — a swirling kaleidoscope of vintage leather, handmade crystals, and sizzling halloumi.

As the sun dips low, locals gather on the lawns of Jubilee Park, dogs in tow, to watch the rowers cut through glassy water under the gaze of the Anzac Bridge. Fiercely political yet deeply relaxed, gritty around the edges but undeniably elegant. To live in Glebe is to exist in a permanent state of inner-west cool.

Who lives here

The Old Guard

Activists & Artists

Here since the 70s. The Green Ban was theirs — and so are the terraces that still stand.

The Creative Class

Freelancers & Students

USYD, UTS, culture-first renters paying $1k/week for the privilege of walking everywhere.

The New Professionals

Young Couples

Arriving into $2M+ terraces. They love the soul of Glebe — but are, slowly, why it costs this much.

Resident voice

Graduate Hustler

"Glebe refuses to be gentrified into boredom. It keeps its soul — the swing bridge, the terrace houses, the arguments at the bookshop."

Life in Glebe

2026
01

The Village Vibe

3km from CBD, Glebe feels like a small town. Gleebooks, thrift shops, galleries. The Saturday Markets: a ritual, not an event.

02

Two Dining Cultures

Tramsheds — high-end providores, artisan butchers. Glebe Point Road — affordable Thai, Indian, veg feeding students for decades. Both exist. Neither apologises.

03

The No-Car Life

Light Rail to Central. Ferry to Barangaroo. Walk to CBD in 25–30 min through Wentworth Park. 0.9 cars per household — one of Sydney's lowest.

04

Outdoor Culture

Glebe Foreshore is everyone's backyard. 2.2km to Pyrmont. Rowing at 6am on Blackwattle Bay. Jubilee Park outdoor gym. Dog parking at every cafe.

05

Intellectual Life

Gleebooks events always full. Wednesday trivia at Friend in Hand. Glebe Community Garden. Reading here is a competitive sport.

06

Blackwattle Bay

The new Fish Market has opened. Glebe went from hidden gem to destination. More visitors. World-class seafood. Harbour plazas. Soul still intact — for now.

How they spend their time

2026
06

Evening Rituals

Vinyl nights at Sappho, poetry slams, jazz. Tuesday here is louder than it looks.

01

Water Sports

Glebe Rowing Club est. 1879 — on Blackwattle Bay at 6am. Dragon boats near Fish Market.

02

Running

2.2km Foreshore to Pyrmont. Since 2026: run to Barangaroo without a traffic light.

03

Fitness

Jubilee Park calisthenics. Yoga studios on Glebe Point Rd — sunrise views of Anzac Bridge.

04

Team Sport

Wentworth Park — Glebe Dirty Reds, ultimate frisbee. Parkrun every Saturday.

05

Dog Socialising

At 5pm, Federal Park becomes the suburb's real social mixer. A genuine ritual.

Resident Voices

01 · Urban Singles
Solo Creative Freelancer
Loves it
I can walk to work, a bar, and the best flat white in Sydney in under five minutes.
Frustrates
$1,000 a week for a mouldy room with no parking and three strangers.
02 · Young Professionals
Graduate Hustler
Loves it
Glebe refuses to be gentrified into boredom. It keeps its soul.
Frustrates
The Green Ban saved the terraces but also locked the suburb in amber. Parking hasn't changed since 1975.
03 · Creative Class
Culture-First Renter
Loves it
Every weekend has a festival, a market, a debate. The suburb never asks you to slow down.
Frustrates
Finding a quiet evening is genuinely difficult. Glebe is many things — tranquil isn't one of them.

2026 Update

Blackwattle Bay & Beyond

For a suburb that once organised a union ban to save its own streetscape from a motorway, what is happening now at its borders feels like earned reward. The infrastructure arriving in 2026 is the most significant in a generation.

Fish Market — Open
Anchors the $2B Blackwattle Bay precinct. Glebe went from hidden gem to Sydney destination overnight.
Bays West — Announced
8,500 new homes on Glebe Island centred on the upcoming Bays West Metro Station.
Cycleway — Complete
Bridge Road Cycleway done. A 15km Harbour Walk from Woolloomooloo to Rozelle Bay will pass through Glebe.

Kirribilli now
—
Loading…
🌊
Sydney Harbour
—
Sea conditions
Wind
—
—

Two official government residences on one street — Admiralty House (Governor-General) and Kirribilli House (Prime Minister). The nation's secondary political hub.

Despite its ultra-wealthy reputation, over 50% of residents live alone — primarily in heritage-listed Art Deco apartment blocks rather than mansions.

Name derived from the Aboriginal word Kiarabilli — "good fishing spot." One of Sydney's oldest inhabited places, now home to the most photographed harbour views in the city.

Kirribilli

North Sydney Council

Train · Ferry · Bus

April 2026

Portrait

A walk down Broughton Street feels like stepping into a curated European village that happens to have the world's most famous bridge looming over it. The atmosphere is hushed but high-energy — a dense, beautiful mix of Victorian terraces, Federation homes, and Art Deco apartment blocks.

Mornings belong to the Active Elite — locals in designer activewear grabbing a flat white at Celsius Coffee Co before a commute that involves a ferry ride rather than a freeway.

 

The great contradiction of Kirribilli is its density versus exclusivity. One of the most densely populated suburbs in Australia — yet it maintains an aura of extreme privacy. You are minutes from the CBD, but the only sound you often hear is water slapping against the bridge pylons.

Weekends bring a different energy. The Kirribilli Markets arrive with bohemian flair. Bradfield Park fills with picnic blankets facing what may be the finest urban backdrop on earth.

A village of sandstone and jacarandas where the Prime Minister is your neighbour and the Harbour Bridge is your backyard.

Who lives here

The Power Couple

Empty Nesters

Downsized from a North Shore mansion into a $4M apartment. Value the lock-up-and-leave lifestyle and ferry proximity to the Opera House.

The Corporate Commuter

Senior Professionals

Work in the CBD or North Sydney. Chose Kirribilli for the 10-minute bridge walk and Friday night wine culture on Broughton Street.

The Heritage Guardian

Long-Term Residents

Here 20+ years in rent-controlled or long-owned Art Deco flats. The soul of the suburb — seen at the markets every fortnight.

Life in Kirribilli

2026
01

Broughton Street

The heartbeat — compact, bustling. By day: gourmet delis and florists. By night: intimate wine bars and Italian trattorias. Everything within two blocks.

02

Two Dining Cultures

Everyday — Batch Burgers, a flat white, nothing fussy. Upscale — Sails or The Kirribilli Club with panoramic harbour views. The gap between them is significant.

03

The Boardwalk

Kirribilli to Lavender Bay — flat, scenic, past Luna Park and Wendy Whiteley's Secret Garden. The suburb's outdoor living room. Loved by everyone who finds it.

04

No-Car Ethos

Real residents rarely drive. The ferry is the limousine of the people. The train from Milsons Point runs constantly. 0.8 cars per household — many have none at all.

05

Cultural Life

The Kirribilli Markets (Art, Design & Fashion) maintain a creative edge in an otherwise conservative suburb. Fortnightly. Always crowded. Always worth it.

06

2026 Update

Milsons Point Station upgrade completed late 2025 — full lift access, modernised entry. New micro-bars opened in the hidden lanes behind Broughton Street.

How they spend their time

2026
01

Sailing

Sydney Flying Squadron — local go-to for harbour sailing and harbourside drinks after. Established 1891.

02

The Bridge Loop

Run across the Harbour Bridge to the CBD and back via ferry. ~4–5km. The best commute in the world done twice.

03

Wellness

Yoga on the grass at Jeffrey Street Wharf at sunrise. The dominant local aesthetic — unhurried, deliberate, expensive activewear.

04

Trivia Circuit

The Kirribilli Hotel trivia nights — surprisingly competitive. Lawyers and political staffers. Do not take it lightly.

05

Jacaranda Season

November. McDougall Street turns purple. The whole suburb is photographed. Locals both love and resent the tourists equally.

06

The Sunset Sip

Mary Booth Lookout, a bottle of wine, watching the CBD lights turn on. A Kirribilli ritual with no equal and no substitute.

Resident Voices

01 · Senior Executives
Mark, 45
Loves it
I walk to my office in the CBD in 15 minutes. Best commute in the world. Full stop.
Frustrates
The Jacaranda Tourists in November block the street for Instagram photos. Every. Single. Year.
02 · Creative Class
Elena, 31
Loves it
I can see the Opera House from my kitchen. It's genuinely inspiring to live like this every day.
Frustrates
Finding parking for friends on a Saturday is a complete nightmare. The suburb wasn't designed for cars — and it shows.
03 · Long-Term Residents
John, 72
Loves it
I've been here 30 years. It's still a village. That doesn't happen by accident — people here protect it.
Frustrates
The price of sourdough has become ridiculous. The gentrification is relentless — even the bread isn't safe.

2026 Update

Younger, But Even More Exclusive

Kirribilli is shifting. The influx of high-earning professionals in their 30s is moving the vibe from stuffy heritage to sophisticated harbourside chic. The infrastructure is catching up — and the prices are following.

Milsons Point Station — Complete
Fully upgraded late 2025 — lift access, modernised entry. The gateway to the suburb finally matches its reputation.
Micro-Bars — Open
New small bars in the hidden lanes behind Broughton Street, shifting the evening energy from conservative to curated.
Harbour Shadows — Coming
High-end boutique residential on Holbrook Avenue nearing completion — a new wave of ultra-wealthy residents incoming.
